skill.name: linkerd-patterns
skill.description: Implement Linkerd service mesh patterns for lightweight, security-focused service mesh deployments. Use when setting up Linkerd, configuring traffic policies, or implementing zero-trust networking.
skill.summary: Production-ready Linkerd service mesh patterns for Kubernetes including mTLS, traffic splits, and authorization policies.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill provides Linkerd service mesh documentation and YAML templates. The static findings are false positives: shell commands are example documentation blocks, URLs point to official Linkerd resources, and the pipe-to-shell pattern is the standard installation method from Linkerd's official docs. No malicious intent detected.

medium_findings.title: Shell Commands in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The skill contains shell command examples (e.g., 'linkerd install', 'kubectl apply'). These are documentation/templates, not executable code. No user input is processed or executed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All shell commands are enclosed in markdown code blocks as example documentation. The skill provides templates for users to copy, but does not execute any commands itself.

medium_findings.title: External URLs in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The skill references external URLs including official Linkerd installation script and documentation links. These are legitimate resources from the official Linkerd project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs point to official Linkerd domain (linkerd.io) and run.linkerd.io for installation. No data exfiltration or malicious network behavior detected.

low_findings.title: Example IP CIDR Range
low_findings.description: A sample CIDR range (10.0.0.0/8) is used in a ServerAuthorization policy example. This is a standard private network range used in documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The CIDR 10.0.0.0/8 is a standard RFC 1918 private address range used in examples. It is not a real network address.

content.value_statement: This skill provides production-ready patterns for deploying and configuring Linkerd service mesh on Kubernetes, enabling automatic mTLS, traffic management, and zero-trust security policies.
actual_capabilities: Install Linkerd control plane and data plane proxies
Configure automatic mTLS for pod-to-pod encryption
Create ServiceProfiles for per-route metrics, retries, and timeouts
Set up TrafficSplit for canary deployments and A/B testing
Define ServerAuthorization policies for mesh TLS access control
limitations: Requires existing Kubernetes cluster to apply configurations
Does not install or manage Kubernetes itself
Limited to Linkerd-specific features; not a general Kubernetes tool
Does not provide troubleshooting for non-Linkerd networking issues

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Setting Up Service Mesh
use_cases.description: A DevOps engineer needs to secure microservice communications with automatic mTLS and implement traffic splitting for canary deployments.

use_cases.title: Security Engineer Implementing Zero-Trust
use_cases.description: A security engineer wants to enforce zero-trust network policies by restricting traffic between services using Linkerd's ServerAuthorization.

use_cases.title: Platform Team Managing Multi-Cluster
use_cases.description: A platform team needs to extend Linkerd across multiple Kubernetes clusters for high availability and disaster recovery.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Linkerd Installation
prompt_templates.prompt: Install Linkerd on my Kubernetes cluster and verify the installation is working correctly.

prompt_templates.title: Enable Automatic mTLS
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to enable automatic mTLS for all pods in namespace 'my-app' and explain how Linkerd handles certificate rotation.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Canary Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TrafficSplit configuration to route 10% of traffic to 'my-service-canary' and 90% to 'my-service-stable' for canary deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Service Authorization
prompt_templates.prompt: Create ServerAuthorization and Server resources to allow only the 'frontend' service account to access 'my-service' on port 8080 with mesh TLS.

output_examples.input: Install Linkerd on my Kubernetes cluster
output_examples.output:
Here is how to install Linkerd:

1. Install the Linkerd CLI:
   curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSfL https://run.linkerd.io/install | sh

2. Validate your cluster:
   linkerd check --pre

3. Install CRDs:
   linkerd install --crds | kubectl apply -f -

4. Install control plane:
   linkerd install | kubectl apply -f -

5. Verify installation:
   linkerd check

Here is a TrafficSplit configuration for canary deployment:

```yaml
apiVersion: split.smi-spec.io/v1alpha1
kind: TrafficSplit
metadata:
  name: my-service-canary
  namespace: my-namespace
spec:
  service: my-service
  backends:
    - service: my-service-stable
      weight: 900m
    - service: my-service-canary
      weight: 100m
```

This routes 90% to stable and 10% to canary.

output_examples.input: How do I enable automatic retries?
output_examples.output:
Create a ServiceProfile with retry configuration:

```yaml
apiVersion: linkerd.io/v1alpha2
kind: ServiceProfile
metadata:
  name: my-service.my-namespace.svc.cluster.local
spec:
  routes:
    - name: GET /api/users
      condition:
        method: GET
        pathRegex: /api/users
      isRetryable: true
  retryBudget:
    retryRatio: 0.2
    minRetriesPerSecond: 10
    ttl: 10s
```

The retryBudget allows up to 20% additional traffic from retries.

best_practices: Always run 'linkerd check' after any configuration change to verify correctness
Enable ServiceProfiles for all services to get per-route metrics and enable retries
Start with permissive policies and tighten gradually; use Linkerd's visibility to understand traffic patterns first
anti_patterns: Skipping the pre-installation check with 'linkerd check --pre'
Applying configurations without understanding the impact on existing traffic
Setting overly broad ServerAuthorization policies that defeat zero-trust goals

faq.question: What is Linkerd?
faq.answer: Linkerd is a service mesh for Kubernetes that provides automatic mTLS, traffic management, observability, and security policies for microservice communications.

faq.question: Does Linkerd require code changes?
faq.answer: No, Linkerd works by injecting a proxy sidecar into pods. Most features work without any code changes to your applications.

faq.question: How does automatic mTLS work?
faq.answer: Linkerd automatically issues and rotates TLS certificates for all pod-to-pod communications. Services authenticate each other using mutual TLS without any configuration.

faq.question: Can I use Linkerd with ingress controllers?
faq.answer: Yes, Linkerd integrates with ingress controllers like NGINX and Traefik. You may need to mark the ingress as 'unauthenticated' in ServerAuthorization for external traffic.

faq.question: What is a ServiceProfile?
faq.answer: A ServiceProfile is a Linkerd custom resource that defines per-route metrics, retries, timeouts, and traffic splits for a service. It enables advanced traffic management features.

faq.question: How do I monitor Linkerd?
faq.answer: Install the Linkerd Viz extension for built-in dashboards. Use 'linkerd viz top' for live traffic views, 'linkerd viz stat' for metrics, and 'linkerd viz edges' for service dependencies.
